fragments.content: Lymphomas are malignancies of lymphocytes classified as Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) or non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). Hodgkin lymphoma: characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells (CD30+, CD15+, PAX5 weak+); subtypes include classical HL (nodular sclerosis, mixed cellularity, lymphocyte-rich, lymphocyte-depleted) and nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL (NLPHL, CD20+, 'popcorn cells'). Non-Hodgkin lymphoma: diverse group (>60 subtypes). B-cell NHL (85%): diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL, most common, aggressive), follicular lymphoma (indolent, t(14;18) BCL2), mantle cell lymphoma (t(11;14) cyclin D1, aggressive), marginal zone lymphoma (indolent), Burkitt lymphoma (t(8;14) MYC, very aggressive), CLL/SLL. T-cell NHL (15%): peripheral T-cell lymphoma NOS, anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALCL, CD30+, ALK+ or ALK-), angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (mycosis fungoides, Sézary syndrome).

Treatment depends on type, stage, and risk. Classical HL: early stage - ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) x2-4 cycles + involved-site radiation; advanced stage - ABVD x6 cycles or A-AVD (brentuximab vedotin replaces bleomycin, ECHELON-1 trial); relapsed - salvage chemotherapy + autologous transplant; brentuximab vedotin or checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) for refractory. DLBCL: R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone) x6 cycles standard; Pola-R-CHP (polatuzumab) for high-risk (POLARIS); CNS prophylaxis for high-risk sites; CAR-T (axicabtagene, tisagenlecleucel, lisocabtagene) for relapsed/refractory. Follicular lymphoma: asymptomatic low-burden - observation; symptomatic/bulky - rituximab + chemotherapy (bendamustine, CHOP, CVP); rituximab maintenance; lenalidomide + rituximab alternative. Mantle cell: intensive regimens for fit young patients (cytarabine-containing) + autologous transplant; BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) for relapsed. Marginal zone: rituximab ± chemotherapy; H. pylori eradication for gastric MALT lymphoma.

Diagnosis requires excisional lymph node biopsy (preferred over core needle/FNA to preserve architecture). Pathology: immunohistochemistry determines lineage and subtype (B-cell: CD20, CD79a; T-cell: CD3, CD5; HL: CD30, CD15, PAX5); FISH for translocations (MYC, BCL2, BCL6 for 'double-hit' lymphoma); gene expression profiling for some subtypes. Staging: Ann Arbor system with Cotswolds modifications - Stage I (single node region), II (≥2 regions same side of diaphragm), III (both sides of diaphragm), IV (extranodal involvement); A/B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss >10%). PET-CT standard for staging and response assessment (Deauville score 1-5); bone marrow biopsy often unnecessary if PET negative for HL and DLBCL. Prognostic scores: IPI (International Prognostic Index) for DLBCL (age, stage, LDH, performance status, extranodal sites); FLIPI for follicular lymphoma; IPS for advanced HL. LDH elevation indicates high tumor burden.
